truth_seeking_note: Prefer documented agency numbers and models that track observed data. When dynamical models and official high-severity probabilities outrun soft headlines, report the harder numbers in plain language.

doomsday_clock.context: Closest setting in the Clock’s history. Main drivers: nuclear risk (including the end of key arms limits), climate pressure, and risky new technologies.

overall_summary.framing: Baseline template in plain language. Two big global pressures: nuclear risk after major arms limits expired, and a strengthening El Niño that official forecasts (NOAA/CPC, Jul 2026) say is likely to become very strong by late 2026. Replace the local section with facts people in your country can verify. Official national warnings always beat this website.
overall_summary.cumulative_context: Quiet news days do not cancel multi-season El Niño pressure or structural nuclear risk. Local prep still starts with what your national agencies say.

categories.name: Seismic Activity
categories.metrics: Several magnitude-7+ quakes worldwide in early–mid 2026.
categories.cumulative_context: Large quakes keep happening in high-risk regions. You cannot time the next one.
categories.conservative_reading: Local building standards and household readiness matter more than global year-to-year counts.
categories.prep_priority: Know drop-cover-hold. Keep water and basic supplies if you live in a quake zone.
categories.discrepancies: Magnitudes usually agree; damage reports lag.

categories.name: Volcanic Activity
categories.metrics: Several volcanoes on higher alert; temporary tsunami warnings near New Zealand in July 2026.
categories.cumulative_context: Most people are not next to an erupting volcano, but ash and flight disruption can still matter.
categories.conservative_reading: Follow official alerts in volcanic or tsunami-exposed areas.
categories.prep_priority: If ash is in the air, follow health and aviation advice.

recent_events.description: Submarine activity near New Zealand; tsunami warnings later cancelled

categories.discrepancies: Alert timing can differ by agency.

categories.name: Solar & Space Weather
categories.metrics: January 2026: one of the strongest solar radiation storms in decades, plus a major geomagnetic storm (NOAA).
categories.cumulative_context: Big solar storms can affect satellites, radio, and in rare cases power systems.
categories.conservative_reading: Treat strong storms as real operational risks, not only pretty auroras.
categories.prep_priority: Most people need no special action. In rare big storms, GPS or radio can glitch.

recent_events.description: Major solar radiation and geomagnetic storm

categories.discrepancies: Storm grades are standard; specific tech damage is often under-reported.

categories.name: Ocean Temperatures & Marine Heatwaves
categories.metrics: NOAA/CPC (9 Jul 2026): El Niño Advisory; about 97% chance it lasts into early spring 2027; about 81% chance of a very strong peak in Oct–Dec 2026 — among the largest since 1950.
categories.cumulative_context: This is not a mild watch-and-wait story. Official products give high odds of a very strong, multi-season event. Dynamical models that track deep Pacific heat often look more severe than softer statistical averages or lagging media language. Local impacts depend on where you live — but stronger events tilt the odds harder for heat, drought, fire weather, marine stress, and food prices in many regions.
categories.conservative_reading: Truth-seeking planning case: treat a very strong multi-season El Niño as the baseline expectation, not a fringe tail. Cite CPC’s published probabilities; note when models that assimilate ocean heat are warmer than soft headlines.
categories.prep_priority: Follow your national climate outlook. Prepare for the local teleconnection pattern your country usually sees in strong El Niño years.

recent_events.description: NOAA/CPC ENSO discussion: high chance of a very strong late-2026 El Niño

categories.sources_compared: NOAA/CPC ENSO Diagnostic Discussion (9 Jul 2026), IRI plumes, national met services
categories.discrepancies: Dynamical models that track subsurface heat are often more severe than statistical models. Soft public language can lag the same agencies’ numbers.

categories.name: Sea Level Rise & Coastal Extremes
categories.metrics: Seas continue rising by a few millimetres a year on average.
categories.cumulative_context: The everyday effect is more minor flooding on high tides — not a single movie-style flood day.
categories.conservative_reading: Adaptation and insurance matter more than panic.
categories.prep_priority: If you are coastal, know local flood maps and insurance gaps.

categories.name: Weather Extremes, Drought & Heat Stress
categories.metrics: Early 2026 already brought severe heat in several regions. A very strong El Niño raises odds of more heat and drought where teleconnections point that way.
categories.cumulative_context: Heat is already hurting people in many places. The El Niño path adds pressure into 2027.
categories.conservative_reading: Plan for more heat and dry spells where your national outlook says so.
categories.prep_priority: Have a heat plan. Follow official heat and drought warnings.
categories.discrepancies: Local records are firm; one event is not the whole climate story.

categories.name: Wildfire Activity & Air Quality
categories.metrics: Severe early-2026 fires in several countries under hot, dry conditions.
categories.cumulative_context: Dry fuels and heat raise fire danger. Smoke can harm people far from the flames.
categories.conservative_reading: Treat fire-season readiness as normal work in fire-prone areas.
categories.prep_priority: Written leave-early plan if you live near wildland fuels. Masks for smoke.
categories.discrepancies: Burned-area totals change through the season.

categories.name: Military Conflict & Escalation Risks
categories.metrics: Major wars ongoing; nuclear threats have been used in politics for years.
categories.cumulative_context: Overseas wars can still hit fuel and food prices everywhere.
categories.conservative_reading: Conflict risk is not only local to the battlefield.
categories.prep_priority: Expect possible price swings. Stick to reputable news.
categories.discrepancies: Analysts disagree on escalation odds; the fighting is documented.

categories.name: Near-Earth Objects (NEOs)
categories.metrics: No serious near-term asteroid impact on public watch lists.
categories.conservative_reading: No public action needed for impact risk now.
categories.prep_priority: No action needed for impact risk right now.

categories.name: Geomagnetic Field Stability & Core Dynamics
categories.metrics: Earth’s magnetic field is slowly weakening; magnetic poles keep moving.
categories.cumulative_context: This is not an imminent pole-flip disaster. It mainly affects satellites and long-term grid planning.
categories.conservative_reading: Slow infrastructure issue, not a household emergency.
categories.discrepancies: Scientists agree the field is changing; they do not predict an imminent full flip.

categories.name: Infrastructure & Financial Strain
categories.metrics: Stacked climate, conflict, and tech pressure raises costs for ordinary life.
categories.cumulative_context: When heat, fire, and global shocks stack up, food, power, and insurance get harder.
categories.conservative_reading: Buffers beat predicting the next single headline.
categories.prep_priority: If you can, keep a small buffer of water, food, medicine, and cash.
categories.discrepancies: Official numbers lag what households feel.

categories.name: Nuclear Risk
categories.metrics: Doomsday Clock at 85 seconds. Last major US–Russia arms-limit treaty expired in February 2026 with no replacement.
categories.cumulative_context: This is the main reason the Clock is so close to midnight. Ordinary prep cannot solve it.
categories.conservative_reading: Nuclear risk is the leading global catastrophe pathway on this template. Politics and arms control are the real levers.
categories.prep_priority: You cannot fully prep for nuclear war. Support serious arms-control politics. Ignore social-media nuclear rumours.

recent_events.description: New START arms treaty expires with no replacement
recent_events.impact: No verified limits between the two largest nuclear arsenals

categories.sources_compared: Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists and arms-control research
categories.discrepancies: Experts disagree on exact odds of use; they agree structural risk is high.
